coredata.dc:title: Silicon nanoparticles-based ratiometric fluorescence platform: Real-time visual sensing to ciprofloxacin and Cu2+
coredata.prism:publicationName: Spectrochimica Acta Part A: Molecular and Biomolecular Spectroscopy
coredata.dc:creator: Yuan, Xiaoying
Lv, Wendi
Wang, Bing
Yan, Chenglu
Ma, Qiuting
Zheng, Baozhan
Du, Juan
Xiao, Dan
coredata.dc:description: In this work, a silicon nanoparticles (Si NPs)-based ratiometric fluorescence sensing platform was conveniently fabricated by simply mixing fluorescent Si NPs as co-ligands and reference signal with lanthanide metal ion Eu3+ as response signal. The introduction of ciprofloxacin (CIP) remarkably turned on the characteristic fluorescence of Eu3+ at 590 nm and 619 nm through the “antenna effect”. At the same time, the blue emission of Si NPs at 445 nm kept comparatively stable. A good linear relationship between the ratio fluorescence intensity and CIP concentration in the range of 0.211–132.4 μM with a limit of detection (LOD) of 89 nM was obtained. In the presence of Cu2+, the fluorescence emission of Eu3+ was sharply turned off because of the stronger coordination ability of Cu2+ with CIP, which guaranteed the sequential detection of Cu2+. Meanwhile, the distinct fluorescent color change from bright blue to red, then back to blue, enabled naked-eye visual detection of CIP and Cu2+ in the solution phase and on paper-based test strip, and was successfully applied to determine the levels of CIP in complicated food samples with high sensitivity.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Silicon nanoparticles
Eu3+
Ratiometric fluorescence
Ciprofloxacin
Cu2+

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Fluorescence detection of CIP and Cu2+.
Determination of CIP in food samples.
Fabrication of the paper-based visual sensor
Characterization of the fluorescent Si NPs and Si NPs-Eu3+
Highly sensitive ratiometric detection of CIP
Possible mechanism of the fluorescent response to CIP

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency-matched-string: National Natural Science Foundation of China

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work is financially supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (No.21876117, U1833124 and U1833202). We also appreciate Analytical & Testing Centre of Sichuan University for the characterizations.

This work is financially supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (No.21876117, U1833124 and U1833202). We also appreciate Analytical & Testing Centre of Sichuan University for the characterizations.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (A) the fluorescent excitation spectrum of Si NPs and emission spectra of Si NPs, Eu3+ and Si NPs-Eu3+. (Inset: Images of Si NPs and Si NPs-Eu3+ solution under UV light (λ = 365 nm); (B) HR-TEM images of Si NPs; (C) XPS survey spectrum of Si NPs; (D) Zeta potentials of the Si NPs and Si NPs-Eu3+; (E) FTIR spectrum of Si NPs and Si NPs-Eu3+.

(A) The fluorescent spectra of Si NPs, Eu3+, Si NPs-Eu3+ and Si NPs-Eu3+-CIP; (B) The fluorescence intensity ratio (I 619/I 445) of the Si NPs-Eu3+ responses toward tested substances including STR, CHL, PEN, TRY, TRI, ENO, NOR, FLE, TC and CIP.

Fluorescence spectra of Si NPs-Eu3+ with various concentrations of CIP (A); linear equation of I 619/I 445 versus the concentrations of CIP (B). Images of the Si NPs under UV light (λ = 365 nm) with different concentrations of CIP (C); FTIR spectrum of CIP and Si NPs-Eu3+-CIP (D).
UV–vis absorption spectra of CIP, Si NPs-Eu3+ and Si NPs-Eu3+-CIP.

Fluorescent spectra change of the Si NPs-Eu3+-CIP solution upon addition of different concentrations of Cu2+ (0–398 μM) (A); Linear relationship of fluorescence intensity (I 619 /I 445) against the concentration of Cu2+ (B). Images of the system under UV light (λ = 365 nm) with different concentrations of Cu2+ (C).

Photographs of the corresponding color changes of the filter paper strip after the above treatment under 365 nm UV light. The concentrations of CIP are 0, 0.15, 0.25, 0.5, 1, 2, 2.5 mM, respectively. From (a) to (f) are including the blank colorimetric chart and the application on the chicken, fish, pork, egg and honey samples.
Illustration of Si NPs-Eu3+ ratiometric FL probe for CIP detection.
Detection of CIP in different food samples.

head.ce:title: Silicon nanoparticles-based ratiometric fluorescence platform: Real-time visual sensing to ciprofloxacin and Cu2+

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Chemistry, Sichuan University, Chengdu 610064, China
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: College of Chemistry, Sichuan University, Chengdu 610064, China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Key Laboratory of Green Chemistry and Technology, Ministry of Education, Sichuan University, Chengdu 610064, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Key Laboratory of Green Chemistry and Technology

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Key Laboratory of Green Chemistry and Technology, Ministry of Education, Sichuan University, Chengdu 610064, China

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding authors at: College of Chemistry, Sichuan University, Chengdu 610064, China.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: In this work, a silicon nanoparticles (Si NPs)-based ratiometric fluorescence sensing platform was conveniently fabricated by simply mixing fluorescent Si NPs as co-ligands and reference signal with lanthanide metal ion Eu3+ as response signal. The introduction of ciprofloxacin (CIP) remarkably turned on the characteristic fluorescence of Eu3+ at 590 nm and 619 nm through the “antenna effect”. At the same time, the blue emission of Si NPs at 445 nm kept comparatively stable. In the presence of Cu2+, the fluorescence emission of Eu3+ was sharply turned off because of the stronger coordination ability of Cu2+ with CIP. Meanwhile, the distinct fluorescent color change from bright blue to red, then back to blue, enabled naked-eye visual detection of CIP and Cu2+ in the solution phase and on paper-based test strip.

In this work, a silicon nanoparticles (Si NPs)-based ratiometric fluorescence sensing platform was conveniently fabricated by simply mixing fluorescent Si NPs as co-ligands and reference signal with lanthanide metal ion Eu3+ as response signal. The introduction of ciprofloxacin (CIP) remarkably turned on the characteristic fluorescence of Eu3+ at 590 nm and 619 nm through the “antenna effect”. At the same time, the blue emission of Si NPs at 445 nm kept comparatively stable. A good linear relationship between the ratio fluorescence intensity and CIP concentration in the range of 0.211–132.4 μM with a limit of detection (LOD) of 89 nM was obtained. In the presence of Cu2+, the fluorescence emission of Eu3+ was sharply turned off because of the stronger coordination ability of Cu2+ with CIP, which guaranteed the sequential detection of Cu2+. Meanwhile, the distinct fluorescent color change from bright blue to red, then back to blue, enabled naked-eye visual detection of CIP and Cu2+ in the solution phase and on paper-based test strip, and was successfully applied to determine the levels of CIP in complicated food samples with high sensitivity.

ce:section.ce:para: Silicon nanoparticles (Si NPs), a novel type of photoluminescence nanoparticles, have attracted considerable attention in the chemical and biological fields due to their superior merits, including good optical properties, facile synthetic routes, low cost, storage stability, surface tailorability, excellent biocompatibility and low cytotoxicity [1,2]. These advantages make Si NPs superior to classic fluorescent molecules and toxic heavy metal-containing quantum dots such as CdTe QDs [3]. In the past few years, the researches of fluorescent nanoprobes based on Si NPs were mainly focused on fluorescence detection and imaging [4–6]. Li et al. reported a water-soluble Si QDs for selective Zn2+ sensing [7]. Chen and coworkers proposed a Si NPs-based dual-responsive ratiometric probe for selective hypochlorite sensing and cell imaging [8]. Recently, we have also developed Si NPs-based fluorescent sensing assay for the simultaneous discrimination and detection of catechol and hydroquinone [9]. Therefore, Si NPs can be highly exploitable in the development of novel optical sensors.

Nowadays, lanthanide-based fluorescent probes have been concerned in many research fields because of their unique optical properties, including long fluorescence lifetime, high luminescence quantum yield, large Stokes and/or anti-Stokes shifts and sharp line-like emission bands. The research of fluorescence sensing platforms based on lanthanide coordination nanoparticles has been designed for a series of targets detection [10,11], diagnosis [12,13] and bioimaging [14,15]. In particular, Eu3+-based coordination nanoprobes have the excellent red fluorescence emission characteristic, which is helpful to distinguish from background fluorescence and reduce the interference of autofluorescence in biological samples. These advantages make the Eu3+-based fluorescence probes particularly suitable for visual detection in practical applications. Rong et al. have presented europium-doped carbon dots synthesized by citric acid and europium nitrate as fluorescent probe to detect DPA on a test stripe. This method effectively utilized the red characteristic fluorescence of Eu3+ and realized the visual and sensitive detection of DPA [16]. Chen’s group have synthesized Eu-doped silicon nanoparticles via stirring APTES, AA and EuCl3·6H2O mixture for 10 h to monitor anthrax spore biomarker-dipicolinic acid [17]. In spite of the satisfactory detecting results, the obvious disadvantage of tedious preparation process in this method can’t be neglected. Chu and coworkers have realized the long-term and real-time measurement of pH in live cells by using europium-doped silicon nanorods (Eu@SiNRs) as ratiometric fluorescent probe. In this sensing system, the emission peak of SiNRs at 470 nm was pH sensitive, and that of Eu3+ at 620 nm was pH insensitive [18].

Ciprofloxacin (CIP), as one of the most important fluoroquinolone antibiotics, has been widely applied as veterinary and human medicines to fight against bacterial infections, playing an important role in human health and many other fields due to the advantages of powerful therapeutic effect, good oral absorption, relatively low toxicity and cheap price [19]. However, the irrational abusing of CIP will lead to residue spread through the food chain, and finally accumulate in the human body, resulting in immediate adverse effects on human beings such as gastrointestinal complaints and central nervous system disorders [20]. Additionally, CIP can cause joint injury in the development stage, which is not suitable for pregnant women or children [21]. Thus, the determination of CIP has attracted much attention, especially in blood serum [22], urine [23] and environmental water [24]. However, the researches on the detection of CIP residues in food are rarely reported, especially in our daily food, such as fish, beef, chicken, pork, eggs and honey et al. Consequently, it is still highly desirable to construct a convenient, practical and reliable strategy for the detection of CIP in food samples, which is of great significance for guaranteeing environmental and food safety, as well as human health.

To date, various methods have been developed for the detection of CIP, including high-performance liquid chromatography (HPLC) [25], capillary electrophoresis [26], chemiluminescence [27], colorimetry [28], electrochemical techniques [29] and fluorescence [22]. Compared with other methods, fluorescence method has obvious advantages due to their simplicity, high-speed, high sensitivity, low-cost and non-destruction. Moreover, based on determining the intensities of two different emission wavelengths at the same time under same excitation wavelength to obtain the intensity ratio, ratiometric fluorescence assays can efficiently reduce fluorescence background interference from instrumental or environmental factors, which provides more accurate measurement in analytical applications, especially for complex samples [30–32].

Copper is one of the trace elements essential for human health. However, exposure to high concentrations of copper can lead to the disturbance of the cellular homeostasis, which will cause serious diseases. Therefore, the US Environmental Protection Agency (EPA) has set the maximum allowable limit of copper in drinking water to 1.3 ppm (~20 μM) [33]. Nevertheless, due to the wide use of Cu2+ in agriculture and industry, copper contamination and the detection continue to be challenging problems. In recent years, the methods that have been reported to detect copper are usually complicated, time-consuming and expensive [34,35]. Accordingly, it is of great significance to develop a simple and rapid fluorescent probe for Cu2+ determination.

In consideration of these above factors, herein, we developed a novel strategy to propose a dual-emitting ratiometric fluorescent probe based on Eu3+ functionalized Si NPs for selective and sequential detection of CIP and Cu2+. Si NPs were easily synthesized by one-step hydrothermal method. Without any further synthesize and post-treatment process, only by simply mixing the obtained blue fluorescence Si NPs with Eu3+, the fluorescence ratiometric sensing platform was then developed. When CIP was added to the system, the red fluorescence of Eu3+ was turned on significantly (Scheme 1 ), and the good linear relationship between the ratio fluorescent intensity and CIP concentration insured the sensitive analysis of CIP in food samples. In the presence of Cu2+, the fluorescence of Eu3+ was sharply turned off because of the stronger coordination ability of CIP with Cu2+ than that with Eu3+. Therefore, the Si NPs-Eu3+-CIP platform has also been successfully applied to the recognition of Cu2+. With the addition of different concentrations of CIP and Cu2+, the distinct fluorescence color changing of the system from blue to red, then back to blue, can be easily recognized by the naked eye, which laid the foundation of visual detection strategy for CIP and Cu2+ by means of a paper-based test strip.

Ciprofloxacin (CIP), tetracycline (TC), fleroxacin (FLE), norfloxacin (NOR), enoxacin (ENO), erythromycin (TRY), penicillin (PEN), chloramphenicol (CHL), streptomycin sulfate (STR) and europium nitrate were obtained from Dalian Meilun Biotechnology Co., Ltd. Trimethoprim (TRI) was bought from Yuanye Biological Technology Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, China). Disodium ethylenediamine tetraacetate (EDTA) was purchased from Chengdu Chemical Reagent Factory. 3-Aminopropyl trimethoxysilane (APTMS) was supplied by Shanghai Titan Scientific Co., Ltd. NaCl, KCl, MgCl2, CaCl2, Al(NO3)3, MnCl2, Ni(NO3)2, CuSO4 and FeCl3 were obtained from Tianjin Komeo Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd. All chemicals used were analytical reagents without further purification.

All optical properties analysis was collected with UV–Vis spectrophotometer (Hitachi, UV-2900, Tokyo, Japan) and fluorescence spectrophotometer (Hitachi, F-7000, Tokyo, Japan). FT-IR spectra were acquired on Thermo Scientific Nicolet 6700 FT-IR spectrometer (Sugar Land, TX, USA). Scanning electron microscopy (SEM) images were recorded on a field emission Hitachi S4800 microscope (Japan) equipped with an Oxford Energy Dispersive Spectrometer (EDS) detector (UK) to perform the elemental analysis. X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) spectra were performed on a Kratos XSAM 800 spectrometer (Manchester, UK) with Mg Kα radiation (hν = 1253.6 eV). The morphology of the Si NPs was measured on a field transmission electron microscopy (TEM, JEOL 200 kV JEM-2100F, Japan). Fluorescence lifetime was collected with a Fluorolog-3 spectrofluorometer (Horiba JobinYvon) with a DeltaDiode as the excitation source and a picosecond photon detection module (PPD-850) as the detector.

The blue-emissive Si NPs were prepared based on a reported method [9]. In a typical experiment, 1.96 g EDTA was dispersed in 40 mL Ar saturated aqueous solution, then 10 mL APTMS was added. After stirring for 2 min, the obtained solution was placed in a polytetrafluoroethylene autoclave for 200 ℃ and reacted for 6 h. The purification of the Si NPs was conducted through a dialysis membrane (1000 Da) for about 24 h. The resulting material was further treated by freeze-drying to obtain Si NPs powder, which were dispersed in water for the following experiments.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fluorescence detection of CIP and Cu2+.
ce:section.ce:para: For detection of CIP, 1.0 mL of HEPES buffer solution (10 mM, pH = 7.9), 7 μL Si NPs (0.16 mg mL−1) and 180 μL of aqueous Eu(NO3)3 solution (10 mM) were mixed together, which was denoted as Si NPs-Eu. Different volumes of CIP solutions of 0.5, 2 and 10 mM were added to the above solutions, with a final concentration in the range of 0.211 to 312 μΜ. After reacting for 30 s at room temperature, the fluorescence spectra were recorded from 380 nm to 650 nm at λex = 360 nm. The emission intensity ratio of Si NPs-Eu at 619 nm and 445 nm (I 619 /I 445) was used for the quantitative analysis of CIP.

For recognition of Cu2+, different volumes of Cu2+ solutions of 2 and 10 mM were added to the above solutions containing 125 μM CIP to get a final concentration in the range of 1.66 to 640 μΜ. After that, the fluorescence intensities of the mixed solution at 619 nm and 445 nm (I 619 /I 445) were recorded with excitation wavelength at 360 nm. In the same way, selectivity experiment, condition optimization, interference study and real sample detection were carried out. All of the experiments were repeated at least three times at room temperature.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Determination of CIP in food samples.
ce:section.ce:para: The proposed fluorescence sensing method was applied to evaluate the feasibility of CIP detection in food samples, including ten kinds of food samples (duck meat, pork, chicken, fish, mutton, beef, egg, milk, milk powder and honey). All the pre-treatment procedure referred to National Standard. According to GB 29692–2013 and GB T 20757–2006, the pre-treatment of milk/milk powder and honey were carried out, respectively. GB/T 20366–2006 was carried on meat, including duck meat, pork, chicken, fish, mutton and beef. After that, the processed samples were mixed with 5 μΜ and 50 μΜ CIP, and the further analysis was the same as that mentioned above.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fabrication of the paper-based visual sensor
ce:section.ce:para: 70 μL of the Si NPs-Eu3+ sensor solution with or without food samples was dropped on the small piece of the filter paper, which was dried in 80 ℃ oven. Then, 50 μL CIP with different concentrations were dropped onto the obtained test strips, dried again in an 80 ℃ oven. The fluorescence color was then observed and photographed under a 365 nm hand-held UV lamp. 50 μL Cu2+ with different concentrations were dropped onto the obtained Si NPs-Eu3+-CIP test strips, which were dried and photographed again to obtain fluorescent photos.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Characterization of the fluorescent Si NPs and Si NPs-Eu3+
ce:section.ce:para: The maximum fluorescence emission intensity of the as-prepared Si NPs at 455 nm can be obtained with an excitation wavelength at 360 nm (Fig. 1 A). Meanwhile, the water solution of Si NPs exhibitted bright blue fluorescence under excitation of 365 nm UV light (inset of Fig. 1A). The emission quantum yield of Si NPs was calculated to be 21.86% by using quinine sulfate (Φ R = 0.54) solution as a reference (Fig. S1). After mixed with Eu3+, neither the fluorescence emission wavelength nor the fluorescence color of the solution showed significant changes (inset of Fig. 1A).

The morphology characteristic of the Si NPs was investigated by high resolution transmission electron microscopy (HR-TEM) and the obtained image was shown in Fig. 1B. It can be clearly seen that the obtained Si NPs had good uniformity and dispersion with the average size about 3.5 nm (Fig. S2). At the same time, The HR-TEM showed high crystallinity of the Si NPs with a lattice parameter of 0.21 nm (inset of Fig. 1B), agreeing with the (220) lattice planes of crystalline Si [36–37].

The peaks at 1130 cm−1 and 1031 cm−1 in the FT-IR spectrum of Si NPs (Fig. 1E) were assigned to the stretching vibration of Si-O bond [38]. The absorption peak at about 1644 cm−1 was ascribed to the bending vibration of N-H [39]. The peak around 2932 cm−1 and 1392 cm−1 indicated the stretching vibration of C-H bond. Meanwhile, the broad absorption band at 3437 cm−1 was attributed to the stretching vibrations of N-H and O-H, indicating the hydroxyl groups and amino groups on the surface of Si NPs, which implied the excellent aqueous solubility and stability of Si NPs and will conducive to covalent conjugation with Eu3+ ions [17,38]. Compared with Si NPs, the FT-IR spectrum of Si NPs-Eu3+ showed stronger absorption intensity and slight shift at 2940 cm−1 and 1384 cm−1, which further confirmed the coordination of Eu3+.

The full-scan XPS spectrum of Si NPs were determined to explore the composition and chemical bonds of Si NPs. Four peaks (Fig. 1C) at 102.9, 284.9, 399.9 and 531.9 eV proved the existence of Si2p, C1s, N1s and O1s, respectively. The peaks in high-resolution C 1s spectrum (Fig. S3A) confirmed the existence of C-Si (284.2 eV), C-C (284.8 eV), C-N (285.9 eV), and C-O (288.4 eV) bonds on the surface of Si NPs. Simultaneously, the peaks at 400.1 eV and 401.2 eV in the N 1s (Fig. S3B) spectrum could be assigned to N-C and N-H groups. Three peaks at 531.2 eV, 532.3 eV and 533.5 eV were observed in O 1 spectrum (Fig. S3C), demonstrating the existence of C=O, C-O-C, and Si-O groups, respectively. The high-resolution Si 2p XPS spectrum of Si NPs (Fig. S3D) demonstrated the presence of Si-O group (102.6 eV and 103.2 eV).

Meanwhile, to identify the main components of the obtained Si NPs, elemental analysis was performed. The result in Fig. S4 showed that Si element accounted for a comparatively large part of the element weight in Si NPs, demonstrating that Si NPs was successfully prepared. Moreover, as shown in Fig. 1D, the zeta potential of −34.9 mV confirmed that the Si NPs had negative charges on surfaces, which was in accordance with the FT-IR results. As comparison, Si NPs-Eu3+ exhibited significant increase of the potential with ζ = −2.58 mV, indicating the successful incorporation of Eu3+ into Si NPs.

As shown in Fig. 2 A, the fluorescence emission spectra of Si NPs, Eu3+, Si NPs-Eu3+ and Si NPs-Eu3+-CIP have been measured under the excitation of 360 nm. Specifically, Si NPs showed an obvious fluorescence emission peak at 445 nm and had little change after adding Eu3+, indicating that Si NPs just acted as ancillary ligands to Eu3+ in the mixing system. Hence, Eu3+ can’t be effectively sensitized by the surface ligands of Si NPs to display its characteristic red fluorescence. But remarkably, the fluorescence intensity at 590 nm and 619 nm sharply increased with the further addition of CIP, accompanying by the fluorescence color of the mixed solution changing from blue to bright red (inset of Fig. 2A). CIP can act as an ‘antenna ligand’ to coordinate with Eu3+ through the oxygen atoms of the carboxy and benzoquinonyl, resulting in the Eu3+ fluorescence turned on. Meanwhile, the blue fluorescence emission of Si NPs at 445 nm kept stable in the presence of CIP. Based on the above results, a sensitive ratiometric CIP sensor based on Si NPs-Eu3+ was proposed effectively.

Selectivity is another crucial parameter to evaluate the performance of a fluorescent probe. Under the same conditions, nine analogues of antibiotics (STR, CHL, PEN, TRY, TRI, ENO, NOR, FLE and TC) were adopted to evaluate the specificity of the developed ratiometric fluorescence method for CIP. The results were shown in Fig. 2B. In ten of the antibiotic analogues, only the addition of CIP generated a dramatic change in the ratio of fluorescence intensity (I 619/I 445), owning to the exclusive coordination and light-harvesting capacity of CIP to open the fluorescence of Eu3+. In contrast, other antibiotics induced negligible influence on the ratio of I 619/I 445 of Si NPs-Eu3+ at the same dose (75 μΜ). The results indicated that the Si NPs-Eu3+ sensing platform provided high selectivity for CIP.

In order to obtain optimal conditions for detection of CIP, several related factors, such as the concentration of Eu3+ and Si NPs, pH value, reaction time and ionic strength were investigated in this assay (Fig. S5). The effect of Eu3+ concentration was first investigated. As shown in Fig. S5A, the ratio fluorescence intensity (I 619/I 445) progressively increased with increasing concentrations of Eu3+ at a Si NPs concentration of 1.2 mg mL−1. A maximum ratio of fluorescence intensity was obtained when Eu3+ concentration reached 1.5 mM, which was selected as the optimal concentration in the following experiments. Then, the influence of Si NPs concentration on CIP detection was studied in the presence of Eu3+ (1.5 mM), and the result (Fig. S5B) revealed that the ratio of I 619/I 445 increased with increasing concentration of Si NPs and reached the maximum at 0.91 mg mL−1 of Si NPs. While excessive Si NPs was a disadvantage for the sensitive detection of CIP, 0.91 mg mL-1Si NPs was chosen for the subsequent experiments.

In the case of a fixed CIP concentration (75 μΜ), the ratio fluorescence intensity of Si NPs-Eu3+ increased gradually in the range of pH = 6.4–7.9(Fig. S5C). This pH-dependent fluorescence property can be attributed to the variation in surface charge of Si NPs owing to protonation-deprotonation. Meanwhile, the decrease of the ratio may be due to the formation of europium hydroxide precipitation under alkaline conditions [10]. Therefore, the pH of 7.9 was chosen in the following experiments.

Effect of reaction time on the fluorescence of the system in the presence of CIP was also explored. The ratio of I 619/I 445 sharply increased within 30 s and reached a plateau (Fig. S5D). Therefore, the reaction time of 30 s was chosen in this detection system. In addition, the influence of different ionic strength on the detection of CIP by Si NPs-Eu3+ was evaluated. The ratio of I 619/I 445 remained unchanged with the increasing concentrations of NaCl (Fig. S5E) from 50 to 300 mM, signifying the strong salt tolerance of the system.

In addition, the as-obtained Si NPs showed no sign of photobleaching even after 2 h of continuous high-power excitation at λex = 360 nm with the xenon lamp (Fig. S5F). The exceptional fluorescence stability of Si NPs has laid a good foundation for their practical applications in complex environments.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Highly sensitive ratiometric detection of CIP
ce:section.ce:para: Under the optimized condition, the ratio fluorescence response of Si NPs-Eu3+ to different concentrations of CIP was assessed and the spectra were shown in Fig. 3 A. With the increasing concentrations of CIP up to 102 μΜ, the characteristic fluorescence emission of Eu3+ at 619 nm concomitantly appeared and enhanced significantly, while the emission peak of Si NPs-Eu3+ at 445 nm remained unchanged under the excitation of 360 nm. It can be noted that the fluorescence intensity at 445 nm slightly enhanced and blue shifted gradually with the concentration of CIP higher than 102 μΜ, which may be attributed to the formation of hydrogen bond between CIP and the groups at Si NPs surface [40]. To confirm this speculation, a control experiment was carried out. As shown in Fig. S6, when there was only Si NPs in the system, the fluorescence intensity increased gradually and emission peak position shifted blue with an increase of CIP concentration, indicating that the fluorescence change at 445 nm was caused by the interaction between Si NPs and CIP. Besides, the FTIR spectrum of the Si NPs-Eu3+-CIP system showed the vibrations of C-OH at about 3422 cm−1 (Fig. 3D, purple line), which red-shifted by 26 cm−1 with respect to that of the initial CIP (3448 cm−1, orange line), indicating the formation of hydrogen bond [22,41].

A good linear relationship between the ratio of I 619/I 445 and concentration of CIP from 0.211 μΜ to 132.4 μΜ was obtained and the limit of detection was calculated to be 89 nΜ. The linear regression equation was I 619/I 445 = 0.0473 + 0.0264c (CIP) (μΜ) with a correlation coefficient 0.9954 (Fig. 3B). The linear range and detection limit of the proposed method were compared with those reported (Table. S1). As can be seen, the sensitivity of this method was comparable to the previously published methods based on nanomaterials. Better still, this strategy has a wider linear range and a simpler preparation process, which makes it more practical.

To evaluate the anti-interference ability of the proposed method, potential interfering substances in the detection of CIP were investigated, including sucrose, glucose, Ca2+, K+, Na+, Cl-, Mg2+, SO4 2-, CO3 2– and NO3 –. The results in Fig. S7 indicated that even at concentrations of 10 times higher than that of CIP, these interfering substances exhibited little influence on I 619/I 445. These results powerfully demonstrated that this probe can be successfully applied to the ratiometric fluorescence response of CIP.

One of the outstanding characteristics of the proposed ratiometric probe is that it can directly detect the dose of analyte under the ultraviolet lamp. As illustrated in Fig. 3C, the blue fluorescence at 445 nm and the sharp increase in the fluorescence intensity at 619 nm led to an obvious fluorescence color change from bright blue to purple, then to red. The sensitive visual response of Si NPs-Eu3+ to CIP shows great practical application potential in real samples.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Possible mechanism of the fluorescent response to CIP
ce:section.ce:para: To investigate the sensing mechanism, the UV–vis absorption spectra of Si NPs-Eu3+, CIP and Si NPs-Eu3+-CIP have been explored (Fig. 4 ). Compared to the absorption spectra of CIP and Si NPs-Eu3+, the peak at 305 nm shifted to 325 nm in Si NPs-Eu3+-CIP, indicating a valid chemical coordination between CIP and Eu3+ on the surface of Si NPs. It has been known that when the coordinated water molecules in lanthanide complex were displaced by ancillary ligands, the emission intensities and lifetimes of lanthanide ions will be enhanced. To gain more insight into the fluorescence turn-on process of Eu3+ induced by CIP, the emission lifetimes of Si NPs, Si NPs-Eu3+ and Si NPs-Eu3+-CIP at 445 nm and 619 nm were measured under 360 nm excitation (Fig. S8). After simply mixing Eu3+ and Si NPs, a very weak fluorescence emission peak at 619 nm appeared with a fluorescence lifetime of 2.62 ns, which may be due to the substitution of the original H2O coordination site of Eu3+ with N and O atoms on the surface of Si NPs at some extent [10]. In addition, the result presented in Fig. S8E showed that the fluorescence lifetime of Si NPs-Eu3+ mixing system was prolonged from 2.62 ns to 0.43 ms at 619 nm and shortened from 4.3 ns to 0.013 ns at 445 nm (Fig. S8C) with the addition of CIP. This may be attributed to the reason that the carboxylic and carbonyl groups in CIP molecules could strongly coordinate with Eu3+ by metal-ligands coordination and ion-pairing interaction, resulting in the intramolecular transfer of fluorescence energy from light-harvesting unit CIP to Eu3+ [17]. Through the “antenna effect” and the 5D0 to 7F J (5D0→7F1 = 590 nm, 5D0→7F2 = 619 nm) electronic transitions of Eu3+, the fluorescence at 590 nm and 619 nm was strongly turned on [42].

It has been reported that CIP molecule, containing a carboxylic acid group, is easy to bind with metal ions, such as Ca2+, Cd2+, Cu2+ and Pb2+, especially with Cu2+ [20,43]. Given this, a new Cu2+-CIP complex can be formed due to the competitive combination between Cu2+ and Eu3+ with the C3-carboxyl and C4-ketone groups of CIP, leading to the dissociation of CIP from Si NPs surface and the change of fluorescence emission. To confirm this speculation, further study was carried out. The binding constant of CIP and Cu2+ (K CIP-Cu) and that of CIP and Eu3+ (K CIP-Eu) were calculated according to the Benesi-Hildebrand equation [42]:
(1)
F 0
Δ F
= α +
α
K
v
n
; α =
1
F -
F 0

The binding constant K was equal to the ratio of the intercept to the slope and the results were calculated to be 5.28 × 109 of K CIP- Cu and 3.5 × 109 of K CIP- Eu (Figure S9), respectively, revealing that Cu2+ displayed more powerful affinity with CIP molecules than Eu3+. Consequently, due to the competitive combination of Cu2+ with CIP over Eu3+ and destruction of antenna effect, the red fluorescence of Eu3+ can be gradually quenched by the addition of Cu2+. This result can further prove that the possible sensing mechanism of Si NPs-Eu3+ to CIP was based on the CIP-sensitized fluorescence turn on of Eu3+.

Based on this, the Si NPs-Eu3+-CIP platform can also be developed toward the ratiometric dual-signal fluorescence sensing and visual recognition of Cu2+. According to Figure S10, various common metal cations (K+, Ca2+, Na+, Mg2+, Al3+, Zn2+, Fe3+, Mn2+ and Ni2+) induced negligible influence on the ratio fluorescence intensity of the system. In contrast, only the addition of Cu2+ generated an obvious decrease of I 619/I 445 at the same dose (200 μM), demonstrating that the recognition effect of Cu2+ is significantly better than other ions. As shown in Fig. S11, upon addition of Cu2+ to the system, the fluorescence intensity at 619 nm decreased obviously within 30 s. The fluorescence emission intensity was very sensitive to the concentration of Cu2+ and the ratio of I 619/I 445 decreased with an increase of Cu2+ concentration (Fig. 5 A). There was a good linear relationship between the ratio fluorescence intensity I 619/I 445 and Cu2+ concentration ranged from 1.66 to 100.0 μM with a regression equation of I 619/I 445 = 3.745–0.0264c (Cu2+) (μM) and a correlation coefficient R 2 = 0.994 (Fig. 5B). The limit of detection was calculated to be 1.2 μM, which is much lower than the allowable limit (~20 μM) in drinking water set by the EPA. At the same time, the blue fluorescence recovery process of the Si NPs-Eu3+ system was also accompanied by a distinguish fluorescence color change from red to blue with an increasing concentration of Cu2+ (Fig. 5C).

To evaluate the feasibility of the as-proposed ratiometric fluorescence probe, the determination of CIP in ten kinds of food samples (duck meat, pork, chicken, fish, mutton, beef, egg, milk, milk powder and honey) was fulfilled. As shown in Table 1 , the recoveries of CIP in different food samples ranged from 95.8% to 106%, and the relative standard deviations (RSD, n = 3) was in normal level, which verified the feasibility and reliability of the ratiometric fluorescent probe based on Si NPs-Eu3+ in food samples.

Furthermore, the filter paper loaded with the as-prepared Si NPs-Eu3+ was further developed to confirm the performance of visual determination of CIP in food samples. As shown in Fig. 6 b, 6c, 6d, 6e and 6f, the color of the filter paper changed significantly from bright blue to red with the increasing concentration of CIP, which was in good agreement with that in aqueous solution system and the colorimetric cards without the addition of real sample in Fig. 6a. With the addition of Cu2+ of increasing concentrations, the fluorescence color gradually changed back to blue (Fig. S12), indicating the great potential of the as-prepared sensor for practical applications.

In summary, by simply mixing Si NPs and Eu3+, a simple, fast, and sensitive ratiometric fluorescence sensing platform for CIP and Cu2+ was conveniently developed and validated. With its high selectivity and sensitivity, the proposed Si NPs-Eu3+ nanosystem was applied to detect CIP in different food samples with satisfactory recoveries. In addition, the fabrication of fluorescence test strips based on Si NPs-Eu3+ provided a new strategy in quantitatively determining CIP. Hence, this Si NPs-Eu3+ sensing platform exhibited a considerable promise for the study of CIP in environmental chemistry and biotechnology.

Xiaoying Yuan: Formal analysis, Methodology, Visualization, Writing - original draft. Wendi Lv: Data curation. Bing Wang: Investigation. Chenglu Yan: Validation. Qiuting Ma: Software. Baozhan Zheng: Project administration. Juan Du: Conceptualization, Funding acquisition, Supervision. Dan Xiao: Resources.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work is financially supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (No.21876117, U1833124 and U1833202). We also appreciate Analytical & Testing Centre of Sichuan University for the characterizations.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.saa.2021.119599.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the Supplementary data to this article:
Supplementary data 1
